suggestion:
Refine toward a clearer, more general framing and evaluation protocol. For example:

"Roundtrip-aware Quantization for Learned Compression Models under Strict Runtime and Artifact Budgets"

Hypothesis: "Staged quantization-aware training (QAT) jointly optimized for both inference-time int8 execution and downstream entropy-coding (e.g., zlib or learned entropy models) can reduce bits-per-byte (bpb) after full roundtrip compression, compared to float-only training and naive post-hoc quantization, under identical compute, runtime (<=1.10x), and model size (<=16MB) constraints."

Concretize with:
1. Task & data: Specify a standard benchmark (e.g., Enwik8 / text, ImageNet/COCO images, or a standard learned image compression dataset) and define val_bpb precisely (post int8 + zlib roundtrip on held-out validation).
2. Baselines: (i) float32 model + post-hoc int8 quantization; (ii) standard QAT not tuned for the roundtrip; (iii) pure zlib/brotli without learned model (where applicable).
3. Method: A staged QAT procedure that explicitly optimizes: (a) quantization robustness, and (b) distributional alignment with the downstream compressor (zlib or a fixed entropy coder), e.g., via differentiable proxies to the roundtrip bpb or learned surrogates approximating the compressor.
4. Constraints: Enforce artifact limit (<=16MB model footprint) and "Quick-gate" (end-to-end runtime <=1.10x a strong float32 baseline) as hard constraints, and report Pareto frontiers between val_bpb and runtime.
5. Generality: Demonstrate that the approach transfers across at least two domains (e.g., text and images, or different compression backends), emphasizing principles for designing roundtrip-aware QAT for arbitrary fixed compressors.

This framing is clearer, more generalizable, and easier to evaluate rigorously, while preserving the original idea of QAT aligned to int8+zlib roundtrip under tight resource budgets.
